text: During the laparoscopic procedure, the surgeon identifies an incarcerated portion of the colon, which is manually reduced. A large defect is visible through the deep inguinal ring.

The robotic-assisted surgery begins with the development of a peritoneal flap, followed by preperitoneal dissection. The surgeon proceeds with medial dissection towards the retropubic space and then continues with lateral dissection.
The surgeon encounters adhesive bands between the colon and the hernia sac, opting to minimize dissection in this area. Some dissection appears to have been performed prior to this clip.
The surgeon begins the medial dissection, identifying the location of the pubis, and then shifts focus laterally to transition into the preperitoneal space.
The surgeon performs an initial dissection, revealing a very thin peritoneum. The surgical field shows the sac, with considerations for potential sac reduction based on its difficulty.
The surgeon inverts the hernia sac using gentle counter tension at the bedside, facilitating further dissection and improving orientation within the surgical field.

The surgeon addresses small fenestrations in the redundant sac while encountering adhesions. The decision is made to abandon the sac, and the surgeon proceeds to lice several adhesions, revealing a pseudosac.
The surgeon establishes a clear view of the myopectenial orifice, indicating progress towards the final stages of the operation.

The surgeon identifies anatomical landmarks and creates a pocket for an extra-large heavyweight mesh. Initial retropubic dissection is performed, along with parietalization of structures and exploration of portions of the hernia sac.
The surgeon positions an extra large heavyweight mesh over the hernia defect in a patient of short stature. The mesh is centered over the defect and fixated at several points.

The surgeon closes the superior flap with ease due to the redundancy in the peritoneum. The hernia sac appears very redundant, and a visible fenestration is noted, which will be straightforward to address.
The surgeon placates the hernia sac, carefully closing the opening to prevent inversion and potential obstruction, while minimizing the sac's size to avoid complications.

The surgeon aggressively integrates the mesh into the peritoneum, using stitches to eliminate dead space and plicate the hernia sac. The stitching technique appears random, covering the area thoroughly.
